figures.caption: FIG. 5. Error analysis for the one-layer network architecture (top row) and the two independent layer architecture (bottom row). Left column: relative error in the real part of the score, εF̃ (k⃗) (Eq. (56)), for different Fourier modes as a function of training time. Right column: Characteristic decay time τ(k⃗) (black dashed line) of the covariance eigenvalue for different modes, C̃(k⃗) = 1/k2. In the one-layer case, the decay time scales linearly with the covariance eigenvalue; in the two-layer case, that scaling is logarithmic. Simulation details: diffusion time t = 0.01, η = 0.01 (top row) and η = 0.005 (bottom row), L = 16, optimization performed using gradient descent over 1024 training configurations, and the average is taken over 500 simulations. Part of the code used for the superstructure of the diffusion model (e.g. the integration algorithm) was adapted from Ref. [81], using a standard machine training implementations via PyTorch convolutions.

FIG. 7. Backward diffusion (denoising) time evolution of the relative error εF , Eq. (71), for a neural network (see text for architectural details) trained on systems of different size L with (left) a fixed kernel size K = 3 and (right) a logarithmically growing kernel, K = 2⌊3/2 log2 L − 2⌋ + 1 (chosen to be always integer and odd).For the former, the peak error around t ≃ 1 grows with L; for the latter, εF is essentially independent of L. The error is controlled and relatively small, even though the training is only performed with a small (10) number of epochs. Error bars denote correspond to ±1 standard deviation. Top inset : Scaling of the kernel size with increasing system size L (scatter points) together with the logarithmic trend line ∼ logL. Bottom inset : First component of the trained score Ft=1;1 at time t = 1 plotted against the first component of the exact score Ft=1;1 for different system sizes. The correlation is nearly perfect.

FIG. B.3. Discretization error for the standard deviation estimate of the k = 2π/L mode of a one-dimensional system at t = 0, ε(t) = σdis(π/L, 0)− σex(π/L, 0). Left column: error behavior for different linear sizes L. Right column: linear coefficients of the fits from the left panel (for a larger set of L) as a function of L. Top row: meff = 0.10−5. Bottom row: meff = 0.5. In the top right plot a power-law fit highlights that the error scales as L, while in the bottom right plot the error saturates given the relatively large value of the mass. Simulation details: lattice spacing a = 1, tmax ≃ 10 (adjusted to be a nearby multiple of δt).

FIG. 4. Critical slowing down in configuration generation. Left: System size dependence of the Frobenius error of the matrix, εC from Eq. (38), scaled by that error for t̄ = 10 and L = 64 (points) for different training times. Results are fitted to the form Ae−C/L2 (dotted line). Right: Same results rescaled as t̄/L2. The ensuing collapse follows the predicted scaling

FIG. 6. Top row: backward diffusion (denoising) time evolution of the relative error εF , Eq. (71), for different system sizes L with (left) fixed kernel size cutoff radius R = 5 and (right) R ∼ logL. For R = 5, the relative error increases with L; for R = logL it saturates at sufficiently large L. Bottom row: peak error of εF (t) (for t ∈ [0.02, 5]) for different L, as a function of (left) the cutoff radius R and (right) the rescaled cutoff radius R/ logL. Setting: d = 2, meff = 0, and a = 1.

FIG. B.2. Error of the trained momentum-space score as a function of the training dataset size M (points) for different forward diffusion times t: L1,1 norm, Eq. (B2), for (a) t = 10−4 and (b) t = 1; Frobenius norm, Eq. (B4) for (c) t = 10−4 and (d) t = 1. The inferred St is obtained by estimating the empirical correlation matrix and then using Eq. (B1) (effectively considering the training time t̄ → ∞). Full vertical lines denote M = N , where N = 2L/a is the number of points that discretize the space. Simulation details: d = 1, a = 0.5, meff = 1.

FIG. C.3. Relative error εF as a function of time for different system sizes L and varying cutoff radius R = 0.2 √ L, d = 1 for (left), fixed cutoff radius R = 3 for (center) d = 3 and (right) d = 4. For a one-dimensional system, the kernel size should grow as √ L in order for the error to remain bounded for increasing system sizes. For d ≥ 3, even if the kernel size remains constant the relative error does not increase with system size. Setting: Effective mass meff = 0, lattice spacing a = 1.

FIG. C.2. Error analysis for (top row) a one-layer network architecture and (bottom row) a single layer repeated twice. Left column: relative error in the real part of the score, εF̃ (k⃗) (Eq. (56)), for different Fourier modes as a function of training time. Right column: Characteristic decay time τ(k⃗) (black dashed line) of the covariance eigenvalue for different modes, C̃(k⃗) = 1/k2. In the one-layer case, the decay time scales linearly with the covariance eigenvalue; in the two-layer case, that scaling is logarithmic. Simulation details: diffusion time t = 0.01, η = 0.01 (top row) and η = 0.005 (bottom row), L = 16, optimization performed using gradient descent over 1024 training configurations, and the average is taken over 500 simulations.

FIG. B.1. Error of the trained real-space score as a function of the training dataset size M (points) for different forward diffusion times t: L1,1 norm, Eq. (B2), for (a) t = 10−4 and (b) t = 1; Frobenius norm, Eq. (B4) for (c) t = 10−4 and (d) t = 1. The inferred St is obtained by estimating the empirical correlation matrix and then using Eq. (B1) (effectively considering the training time t̄ → ∞). Full vertical lines denote M = N , where N = 2L/a is the number of points that discretize the space. Simulation details: d = 1, a = 0.5, meff = 1.

FIG. C.1. Same as Fig. 4, but using the Wasserstein error W 1 instead of the Frobenius error in the covariance matrix. Left: System size evolution of the W 1 error, W1, of the matrix for different training times rescaled by the result for the point (t̄ = 10, L = 64). Results are fitted to the form Ae−C/L2 (dotted line). Right: Same results rescaled as t̄/L2. The ensuing collapse
